planting.note: Transplant into a warm, sunny spot in well-drained or sandy soil after last frost has passed and nighttime temperatures are reliably above freezing.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost date and transplant to a dry, sunny location with sandy or well-drained soil after frost danger has passed. This compact native perennial produces small, sweet-to-tart yellow berries enclosed in papery husks that ripen from midsummer through fall. It is notably more drought-tolerant than tomatillo or cape gooseberry and thrives on neglect once established. Plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies, making them a productive and low-maintenance addition to dryland food gardens or prairie restoration plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Physalis pumila

planting.note: Transplant into sandy, well-drained soil at or just after the last frost date; protect from late cold snaps as young transplants are sensitive to frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost date and transplant to a full-sun site with sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil after frost danger has passed. Native to the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and Texas, this softly hairy perennial ground cherry produces papery-husked yellow berries from late summer through fall that are sweet and pleasant when fully ripe. Plants require excellent drainage and tolerate drought well once established, making them well-suited to xeric southern food gardens. They spread by rhizomes to form loose colonies over time and may die back to the roots in cooler parts of zone 7.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Physalis mollis

planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed into moist, disturbed or garden soil in partial shade to full sun; seeds require warm soil and light to germinate well.

directions: Direct sow onto the soil surface after last frost in partial shade to full sun; seeds need light and warmth to germinate and will often self-sow where established. This widely distributed native annual bears small white star-shaped flowers followed by clusters of small berries that turn fully black when ripe in late summer and fall. CAUTION: green and unripe berries and all other plant parts are toxic and must never be consumed; only correctly identified, fully black, ripe berries have a history of use cooked (never raw) in certain African, Southeast Asian, and indigenous North American culinary traditions. Plants naturalize readily in gardens and disturbed open ground, requiring little care once established.
sources: Native American Ethnobotany Database - Solanum americanum

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seeds will germinate the following spring when soil warms.

directions: Direct sow in fall onto well-drained, rocky or sandy soil in a full-sun location and allow winter weather to provide the cold stratification needed for reliable spring germination. This slender, upright native perennial produces narrow, toothed leaves and long spikes of small pale lilac-pink flowers from May through August that attract native bees, particularly Augochlora bees that specialize on Verbena species. Once established, plants are highly drought-tolerant and long-lived in lean, dry soils with excellent drainage; they are poor performers in rich, moist, or heavy clay soils. Naturalistic prairie and meadow plantings, rock gardens, and dry roadsides are ideal settings for this understated but valuable native.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Verbena simplex

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost into a sunny location; thin seedlings to allow ample room for the tall, branching stems to develop.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in a sunny, open site and thin to 18-24 inches apart; plants grow quickly into tall, stiff-stemmed clumps that need no staking in most conditions. From summer through frost, the candelabra-like branching spikes carry masses of tiny purple-violet flowers that are outstanding butterfly magnets, particularly for skippers, painted ladies, and swallowtails. Cut plants back by half after the first flowering flush to encourage a second wave of bloom and prevent excessive self-seeding. This naturalized South American species self-sows prolifically and can spread widely in open, sunny sites across the Southeast, so site it where volunteer seedlings can be tolerated or remove spent flower heads to manage spread.
